content:
For the two port network shown below, the [Y]-parameters is given as

$$[Y] = {1 \over {100}}\left[ {\matrix{ 2 & { - 1} \cr { - 1} & {4/3} \cr } } \right]S$$

The value of load impedance $$\mathrm{Z_L}$$, in $$\Omega$$, for maximum power transfer will be ___________ (rounded off to the nearest integer).
explanation:
Given two-port network and Y-parameters are given below,

$$ \begin{aligned} & {[Y]=\frac{1}{100}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 2 & -1 \\ -1 & \frac{4}{3} \end{array}\right]} \\\\ & {[Y]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} \frac{2}{100} & \frac{-1}{100} \\ \frac{-1}{100} & \frac{4}{300} \end{array}\right]} \end{aligned} $$

Obtaining $\pi$-network from given $[Y]$ parameters

$$ \begin{aligned} & Y_{11}=Y_A+Y_B=\frac{2}{100} \\ & Y_{12}=Y_{21}=-Y_B=\frac{-1}{100} \\ & Y_{22}=Y_B+Y_C=\frac{4}{300} \end{aligned} $$

From the above equations,

$$ Y_A=\frac{1}{100} S, Y_B=\frac{1}{100} S, Y_C=\frac{1}{300} S $$

Now replacing this pie network in two port network.

For maximum power transfer, $Z_L=Z_{t h}$.

For $Z_{t h}$, circuit becomes.

$$ \begin{aligned} & Z_{t h}=300 \| 109.09=79.99 \Omega \\\\ & Z_{t h}=80 \Omega \end{aligned} $$

For maximum power transfer

$$ Z_L=Z_{t h}=80 \Omega $$

Hence, the correct answer is 80 .

content: In the given circuit, the two-port network has the impedance matrix $[Z]=\left[\begin{array}{cc}40 & 60 \\ 60 & 120\end{array}\right]$. The value of $Z_L$ for which maximum power is transferred to the load is $\_\_\_\_$ $\Omega$.
explanation:
$$ \text { Given two port network is shown in below figure, } $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &[Z]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 40 & 60 \\ 60 & 120 \end{array}\right]\\ &Z \text { parameter equation from matrix is, }\\ &\begin{aligned} & V_1=40 I_1+60 I_2 \\ & V_2=60 I_1+120 I_2 \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

Applying KVL in output loop,

$$ \begin{aligned} & -V_{d c}+120 I_{d c}+60 I_1=0 \\ & V_{d c}=120 I_{d c}+60 I_1 \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)\end{aligned} $$

Applying KVL in input loop,

$$ \begin{aligned} & 0+50 I_1+60 I_{d c}=0 \\ & I_1=\frac{-6}{5} I_{d c} \end{aligned} $$

Substituting the value of $I_1$ in equation (i),

$$ V_{d c}=120 I_{d c}+60\left(\frac{-6}{5}\right) I_{d c} $$

$$ R_{t h}=\frac{V_{d c}}{I_{d c}}=48 \Omega $$

From maximum power transfer theorem,

$$ \begin{aligned} & & Z_L & =Z_{\text {th }} \\ \therefore & & Z_L & =48 \Omega \end{aligned} $$

Hence, the value of $Z_L$ for which maximum power is transferred to the load is $\mathbf{4 8} \boldsymbol{\Omega}$.

$$ \text { Given circuit is shown below, } $$

ABCD matrix for the above network is given as shown below,

$$ \left[\begin{array}{l} V_1 \\ I_1 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} A & B \\ C & D \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{c} V_2 \\ -I_2 \end{array}\right] $$

It can also be written as,

$$ \begin{aligned} & V_1=A V_2-B I_2 \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)\\ & I_1=C V_2-D I_2 \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(ii)\end{aligned} $$

From equation (i),

$$ B=-\left.\frac{V_1}{I_2}\right|_{V_2=0} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(iii)$$

Applying KVL in input loop,

$$ V_1=7 I_1+5 I_2 \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(iv)$$

Applying KVL in output loop,

$$ V_2=5 I_1+7 I_2 \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(v)$$

At $V_2=0$,

$$ \begin{aligned} 0 & =5 I_1+7 I_2 \\ I_1 & =-\frac{7}{5} I_2 \end{aligned} $$

Substituting the value of $I_1$ in equation (iv),

$$ \begin{aligned} V_1 & =7 \times\left(-\frac{7}{5} I_2\right)+5 I_2 \\ V_1 & =-\frac{24}{5} I_2 \\ B & =-\frac{V_1}{I_2}=\frac{24}{5}=4.8 \Omega \end{aligned} $$

Hence, the value of $B$ is $\mathbf{4 . 8} \boldsymbol{\Omega}$.

content: The z-parameter matrix for the two-port network shown is $$$\left[ {\matrix{ {2\,j\,\omega } & {j\,\omega } \cr {j\,\omega } & {3\, + \,2\,j\,\omega } \cr } } \right]$$$ Where the entries are in $$\Omega $$. Suppose $$\,{Z_b}\,\left( {j\,\omega } \right) = {R_b} + j\,\omega $$ Then the value of $${R_b}$$ (in $$\Omega $$) equals _______________________3
explanation:
$$ \text { Given circuit is shown below, } $$

For $T$-type of 2-port network, $z$-parameter can be written as

$$ [Z]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} z_a+z_c & z_c \\ z_c & z_b+z_c \end{array}\right] \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)$$

Given : $[Z]=\left[\begin{array}{cc}2 j \omega & j \omega \\ j \omega & 3+2 j \omega\end{array}\right]\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(ii)$

By comparing equation (i) and (ii),

$$ \begin{aligned} & z_c=j \omega\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(iii) \\ & z_b+z_c=3+2 j \omega \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(iv)\end{aligned} $$

From equation (iii) and (iv),

$$ \begin{aligned} & z_b+j \omega=3+2 j \omega \\ & z_b=3+j \omega=R_b+j \omega \quad \text { [Given] } \\ & R_b=3 \Omega \end{aligned} $$

Hence, the value of $R_b$ is $\mathbf{3} \boldsymbol{\Omega}$.

content:
Consider a two-port network with the transmission matrix: T = $$\begin{bmatrix}A&B\\C&D\end{bmatrix}$$. If the network is
reciprocal, then
explanation:
Given : $T=\left[\begin{array}{ll}A & B \\ C & D\end{array}\right]$

Condition of reciprocal network for $A B C D$ parameter is given by,

$$ \begin{aligned} & |T|=\left|\begin{array}{ll} A & B \\ C & D \end{array}\right|=1 \\ & \Delta T=A D-B C=1 \end{aligned} $$

Hence, the correct option is (D).

Hence, the correct option is (A).

content:
If the transfer function of the following network is $$\,{{{V_0}(s)} \over {{V_i}(s)}} = {1 \over {2\, + \,sCR}}$$

The value of the load resistance $${R_L}$$ is

explanation:
Given : $\left[\begin{array}{ll}A & B \\ C & D\end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll}n & 0 \\ 0 & x\end{array}\right]\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)$

Circuit is shown below,

Standard $\boldsymbol{A} \boldsymbol{B} \boldsymbol{C} \boldsymbol{D}$ parameter equation :

$$ \begin{aligned} & V_1=A V_2-B I_2 \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(ii)\\ & I_1=C V_2-D I_2 \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(iii)\end{aligned} $$

For an ideal transformer,

$$ \begin{aligned} & \frac{V_1}{V_2}=\frac{n}{1} \\ & \frac{I_1}{I_2}=\frac{-1}{n} \end{aligned} $$

In above expression, negative sign is due to direction of output two port current.

$$ \begin{aligned} & V_1=n V_2 \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(iv)\\ & I_1=\frac{-I_2}{n} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(v)\end{aligned} $$

Comparing equation (iv) and (v) with equation (ii) and (iii),

$$ \begin{aligned} & A=n, B=0 \\ & C=0, D=\frac{1}{n} \\ & {\left[\begin{array}{ll} A & B \\ C & D \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} n & 0 \\ 0 & \frac{1}{n} \end{array}\right]} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(vi)\end{aligned} $$

Comparing equation (i) and (vi),

$$ x=\frac{1}{n} $$

Hence, the correct option is (B).

content: If each branch of a Delta circuit has impedance $$\sqrt 3 \,Z$$, then each branch of the equivalent Wye circuit has impedance
explanation:
$Z_A=\frac{Z_{A B} \times Z_{A C}}{Z_{A B}+Z_{B C}+Z_{C A}}$

$Z_A=\frac{\sqrt{3} Z \times \sqrt{3} Z}{\sqrt{3} Z+\sqrt{3} Z+\sqrt{3} Z}=\frac{3 Z^2}{3 \sqrt{3} Z}=\frac{Z}{\sqrt{3}}$

$Z_B=\frac{Z_{a b} \times Z_{b c}}{Z_{a b}+Z_{b c}+Z_{c a}}=\frac{Z}{\sqrt{3}}$

$Z_C=\frac{Z_{b c} \times Z_{c a}}{Z_{a b}+Z_{b c}+Z_{c a}}=\frac{Z}{\sqrt{3}}$

content: A 2-port network is shown in figure. The parameter $${h_{21}}$$ for this network can be given by
explanation:
$$ \text { Given circuit is shown below, } $$

Standard $\boldsymbol{h}$-parameter equation :

$$ \begin{aligned} & V_1=h_{11} I_1+h_{12} V_2 \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)\\ & I_2=h_{21} I_1+h_{22} V_2 \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(ii)\end{aligned} $$

From equation (ii),

$$ h_{21}=\left.\frac{I_2}{I_1}\right|_{V_2=0}=\text { Forward current gain, when } $$

output is short circuited

Applying KVL in loop (1),

$$ \begin{aligned} & I_2 R+\left(I_1+I_2\right) R=0 \\ & 2 I_2 R=-I_1 R \\ & \frac{I_2}{I_1}=\frac{-1}{2}=h_{21} \end{aligned} $$

Hence, the correct option is (A).

content:
The short-circuit admittance matrix of a two-port network is $$$\begin{bmatrix}0&-\frac12\\\frac12&0\end{bmatrix}$$$
The two port network is
explanation:
Given: The admittance matrix is $$[Y]=\begin{bmatrix} 0 & -\frac{1}{2} \\ \frac{1}{2} & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

Symmetrical Network Condition:

The matrix is symmetrical if $$y_{11} = y_{22}$$.
Here, $y_{11} = 0$ and $y_{22} = 0$, so the network is symmetrical.

Reciprocal Network Condition:

The network is reciprocal if $$y_{12} = y_{21}$$.
But here, $y_{12} = -\frac{1}{2}$ and $y_{21} = \frac{1}{2}$, which are not equal.
So, the network is not reciprocal.

Passive or Active:

All passive and resistive networks are always reciprocal.
Since this network is not reciprocal, it must be active (it can have active components).

So, the network is symmetrical, non-reciprocal, and active. The correct answer is option (B).

content: The condition, that a 2-port network is reciprocal, can be expressed in terms of its ABCD parameters as ...............
The open circuit impedance matrix of the 2-port network shown in figure is
explanation:
Given circuit is shown below,

Open circuit impedance parameter $=z$-parameter

Standard $z$-parameter equation :

$$ \begin{aligned} & V_1=z_{11} I_1+z_{12} I_2 .......(i) \\ & V_2=z_{21} I_1+z_{22} I_2 .......(ii) \end{aligned} $$

Applying KVL in loop (1),

$$ \begin{aligned} & V_1=I_2-2 I_1 \\ & V_1=-2 I_1+I_2 .......(iii) \end{aligned} $$

Applying KVL in loop (2),

$$ \begin{aligned} & V_2=2\left(I_2-3 I_1\right)+\left(I_2-2 I_1\right) \\ & V_2=2 I_2-6 I_1+I_2-2 I_1 \\ & V_2=-8 I_1+3 I_2 .......(iv) \end{aligned} $$

Comparing equation (iii) and (iv) with (i) and (ii),

$$ \left[\begin{array}{ll} z_{11} & z_{12} \\ z_{21} & z_{22} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} -2 & 1 \\ -8 & 3 \end{array}\right] $$

Hence, the correct option is (A).

content: For the two port network shown below, the value of the $Y_{21}$ parameter (in Siemens) is ______.
explanation:
Given Circuit is shown below.

$$ \begin{aligned} &\text { Applying KVL in Loop (1), }\\ &\begin{aligned} & -V_1+2 I_1+4\left(I_1+I_2\right)-8 V_1=0 \\ & 6 I_1+4 I_2=9 V_1 \\ & V_1=\left(\frac{6}{9}\right) I_1+\left(\frac{4}{9}\right) I_2 \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)\end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\text { Applying KVL in Loop (2), }\\ &\begin{aligned} & -V_2+4\left(I_1+I_2\right)-8 V_1=0 \\ & V_2=4\left(I_1+I_2\right)-8\left[\left(\frac{6}{9}\right) I_1+\left(\frac{4}{9}\right) I_2\right] \\ & V_2=4\left(I_1+I_2\right)-\frac{48}{9} I_1-\frac{32}{9} I_2 \\ & V_2=\left(4-\frac{48}{9}\right) I_1+\left(4-\frac{32}{9}\right) I_2 \\ & V_2=\left(\frac{-12}{9}\right) I_1+\left(\frac{4}{9}\right) I_2 \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(ii)\end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

From equation (i) and (ii),

$$ \left[\begin{array}{ll} z_{11} & z_{12} \\ z_{21} & z_{22} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} \frac{6}{9} & \frac{4}{9} \\ \frac{-12}{9} & \frac{4}{9} \end{array}\right] $$

Relationship between $Z$ and $Y$ parameter :

$$ \begin{aligned} & {[Y]=[Z]^{-1} \text { and }[Z]=[Y]^{-1}} \\ & {[Y]=[Z]^{-1}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} \frac{6}{9} & \frac{4}{9} \\ \frac{-12}{9} & \frac{4}{9} \end{array}\right]^{-1}} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & {[Y]=\frac{1}{\left(\frac{6}{9} \times \frac{4}{9}+\frac{12}{9} \times \frac{4}{9}\right)}\left[\begin{array}{cc} \frac{4}{9} & \frac{-4}{9} \\ \frac{12}{9} & \frac{6}{9} \end{array}\right]} \\ & {[Y]=\frac{81}{(24+48) 9}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 4 & -4 \\ 12 & 6 \end{array}\right]} \\ & {[Y]=\frac{9}{72}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 4 & -4 \\ 12 & 6 \end{array}\right]=\frac{1}{8}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 4 & -4 \\ 12 & 6 \end{array}\right]} \\ & {[Y]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} \frac{1}{2} & \frac{-1}{2} \\ \frac{12}{8} & \frac{6}{8} \end{array}\right]} \\ & Y_{11}=\frac{1}{2} \mho, Y_{12}=\frac{-1}{2} \mho, Y_{21}=\frac{3}{2}=1.5 \mho \\and\,\,\,\, & Y_{22}=0.75 \mho \end{aligned} $$

Hence, the correct Answer is $\mathbf{1 . 5}$.

content: The h-parameters of a two port network are shown below. The condition for the maximum small signal voltage gain $${{{V_{out}}} \over {{V_s}}}$$ is

The S-parameters of a two port network is given as

$$[S] = \left[ {\matrix{ {{S_{11}}} & {{S_{12}}} \cr {{S_{21}}} & {{S_{22}}} \cr } } \right]$$

with reference to $${Z_0}$$. Two lossless transmission line sections of electrical lengths $${\theta _1} = \beta {l_1}$$ and $${\theta _2} = \beta {l_2}$$ are added to the input and output ports for measurement purposes, respectively. The S-parameters $$[S']$$ of the resultant two port network is
explanation:
$$ \left[\begin{array}{l} V_1^{-} \\ V_2^{-} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} S_{11} & S_{12} \\ S_{21} & S_{22} \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} V_1^{+} \\ V_2^{+} \end{array}\right] $$ ........(i)

To be calculated,

$$ \left[\begin{array}{l} V_{1 N}^{-} \\ V_{2 N}^{-} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} S_{11 N} & S_{12 N} \\ S_{21 N} & S_{22 N} \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} V_{1 N}^{+} \\ V_{2 N}^{+} \end{array}\right] $$

$\begin{aligned} & V_1^{+}=V_{1 N}^{+} e^{-j \theta_1} \\\\ & V_{1 N}^{-}=V_1^{-} e^{-j \theta_1}=V_1^{-}=V_{1 N}^{-} e^{j \theta_1} \\\\ & V_2^{+}=V_{2 N}^{+} e^{-j \theta_2} \\\\ & V_{2 N}^{-}=V_2^{-} e^{-j \theta_2}=V_2^{-}=V_{2 N}^{-} e^{j \theta_2}\end{aligned}$

Feeding the data in equation (i),

$$ \left[\begin{array}{l} V_{1 N}^{-} \\ V_{2 N}^{-} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} S_{11 N} e^{-j\left(2 \theta_1\right)} & S_{12 N} e^{-j\left(\theta_1+\theta_2\right)} \\ S_{21 N} e^{-j\left(\theta_1+\theta_2\right)} & S_{22 N} e^{-j 2\left(\theta_2\right)} \end{array}\right] $$

Hence, the correct option is (A).

When the variable resistance R at Port 2 is replaced by the load shown in Fig. (c), the current I2 is ________ mA (rounded off to one decimal place).
explanation:
For I2 = 0, V2 = VOC = 5 V

For Thevenin's resistance Rth,

For $$-$$I2 = 20 mA, V2 = 0

ISC = $$-$$I2

ISC = 20 mA

Rth = $${{{V_{OC}}} \over {{I_{SC}}}} = {5 \over {20}} \times {10^3} = 250$$ $$\Omega$$

Network is replaced by Thevenin's equivalent,

$${I_2} = {{10 - 5} \over {1.25 \times {{10}^3}}} = {5 \over {1.25}} \times {10^{ - 3}}$$ A = 4 mA

content: For a 2-port network consisting of an ideal lossless transformer, the parameter $S_{21}$ (rounded off to two decimal places) for a reference impedance of $10 \Omega$ is $\_\_\_\_$ .
explanation:
$$ \text { Given : } \quad Z_0=10 \Omega $$

As the given transformer is ideal,

$$ \begin{aligned} & \frac{V_2}{V_1}=\frac{1}{2} \\ & V_1=2 V_2 \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)\\ & \frac{I_1}{I_2}=-\frac{1}{2} \\ & I_1=-0.5 I_2 \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(ii)\\ & {[T]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.5 \end{array}\right]} \end{aligned} $$

Scattering parameter $S_{21}$ in terms of $A B C D$ is given by,

$$ S_{21}=\frac{2}{A+\frac{B}{Z_0}+\frac{C}{Z_0}+D}=\frac{2}{2+0+0+0.5}=\frac{2}{2.5}=0.8 $$

Hence, the parameter $S_{21}$ is $\mathbf{0 . 8}$.

content: The z-parameter matrix $$\begin{bmatrix}z_{11}&z_{12}\\z_{21}&z_{22}\end{bmatrix}$$ for the two-port network shown is
explanation:
$$ \text { Given circuit is shown below, } $$

Standard $z$-parameter equation :

$$ \begin{aligned} & V_1=z_{11} I_1+z_{12} I_2 \\ & V_2=z_{21} I_1+z_{22} I_2 \end{aligned} $$

Given circuit can be redrawn as shown below,

(i) Calculation of $z_{11}$ :

$z_{11}=\left.\frac{V_1}{I_1}\right|_{I_2=0}=$ Driving point input impedance, when output is open circuited.

(ii) Calculation of $z_{21}$ :

$z_{21}=\left.\frac{V_2}{I_1}\right|_{I_2=0}=$ Transfer impedance, when

output is open circuited.

$$ I_{3 \Omega}=\frac{I_1 \times 6}{3+6}=\frac{2}{3} I_1 \quad[\mathrm{By} \mathrm{CDR}] $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &I_{6 \Omega}=\frac{I_1 \times 3}{3+6}=\frac{1}{3} I_1\text { [By CDR] } \end{aligned} $$

Applying KVL in above loop,

$$ \begin{aligned} & -V_2-3 \times \frac{2}{3} I_1=0 \\ & \frac{V_2}{I_1}=-2 \Omega \\ & z_{21}=\frac{V_2}{I_1}=-2 \Omega \end{aligned} $$

(iii) Calculation of $z_{22}$ :

$z_{22}=\left.\frac{V_2}{I_2}\right|_{I_1=0}=$ Driving point output impedance,

when input is open circuited.

$$ \begin{aligned} &I_{3 \Omega}=\frac{I_2 \times 6}{3+6}=\frac{2}{3} I_2\text { [By CDR] } \end{aligned} $$

Applying KVL in above shown loop,

$$ \begin{aligned} & -V_2+\frac{2}{3} I_2 \times 3=0 \\ & z_{22}=\frac{V_2}{I_2}=2 \Omega \end{aligned} $$

(iv) Calculation of $z_{12}$ :

$$ z_{12}=\left.\frac{V_1}{I_2}\right|_{I_1=0}=\text { Transfer impedance, when input is open circuited.} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &I_{6 \Omega}=\frac{3 \times I_2}{3+6}=\frac{3}{9} I_2\text { [By CDR] } \end{aligned} $$

From figure, $-V_1-6 \times \frac{1}{3} I_2=0$

$$ \begin{aligned} & V_1=-6 \times I_2 \times \frac{3}{9}=-2 I_2 \\ & z_{12}=\frac{V_1}{I_2}=-2 \Omega \\ & {[z]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 2 & -2 \\ -2 & 2 \end{array}\right]} \end{aligned} $$

Hence, the correct option is (A).

Standard $\boldsymbol{A} \boldsymbol{B} \boldsymbol{C} \boldsymbol{D}$ parameter equation :

From equation (ii),

Put the value of $I_1$ in equation (i),

$$ \begin{aligned} V_1 & =(7+j 4)\left[\frac{V_2}{2}-\left(\frac{7-j 4}{2}\right) I_2\right]+2 I_2 \\ V_1 & =\left(\frac{7+j 4}{2}\right) V_2-\frac{(7+j 4)(7-j 4)}{2} I_2+2 I_2 \\ V_1 & =\left(\frac{7+j 4}{2}\right) V_2-\frac{(49+16)}{2} I_2+2 I_2 \\ V_1 & =(3.5+j 2) V_2-30.5 I_2 \end{aligned} $$

Comparing equation (vi) and (v) with equation (iii) and (iv),

$$ T=\left[\begin{array}{ll} A & B \\ C & D \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 3.5+j 2 & 30.5 \\ 0.5 & 3.5-j 2 \end{array}\right] $$

content: In the h-parameter model of the 2-port network given in the figure shown, the value of h22 (in S) is _____ .
explanation:
When two networks are connected in parallel, then overall $y$-parameter is addition of individual $y$-parameter.

$$ \text { Standard } \pi \text { - network : } $$

Admittance parameter matrix for standard $\pi$ network is given by,

$$ [Y]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} Y_A+Y_B & -Y_A \\ -Y_A & Y_A+Y_C \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} y_{11} & y_{12} \\ y_{21} & y_{22} \end{array}\right] $$

For network (1) $y$-parameter is given by,

$$ \left[Y_1\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} \frac{1}{3}+\frac{1}{3} & -\frac{1}{3} \\ -\frac{1}{3} & \frac{1}{3}+\frac{1}{3} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} \frac{2}{3} & -\frac{1}{3} \\ -\frac{1}{3} & \frac{2}{3} \end{array}\right] $$

Similarly, for network (2), $y$-parameter is given by,

$$ \left[Y_2\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 1 & -\frac{1}{2} \\ -\frac{1}{2} & 1 \end{array}\right] $$

Thus, $[Y]=\left[Y_1\right]+\left[Y_2\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc}\frac{5}{3} & -\frac{5}{6} \\ -\frac{5}{6} & \frac{5}{3}\end{array}\right]$

$$ \text { } \quad \begin{aligned}\,\, I_1 & =\frac{5}{3} V_1-\frac{5}{6} V_2 \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)\\ and\,\,\,I_2 & =\frac{-5}{6} V_1+\frac{5}{3} V_2 \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(ii)\end{aligned} $$

Standard $\boldsymbol{h}$-parameter equation :

$$ \begin{aligned} & V_1=h_{11} I_1+h_{12} V_2 \\ & I_2=h_{21} I_1+h_{22} V_2 \\ h_{22}= & \left.\frac{I_2}{V_2}\right|_{I_1=0}=\text { Driving point output admittance } \end{aligned} $$

when input is open circuited.

From equation (i),

$$ \begin{aligned} & 0=\frac{5}{3} V_1-\frac{5}{6} V_2 \\ & \frac{5}{3} V_1=\frac{5}{6} V_2 \\ & 2 V_1=V_2 \\ & V_1=\frac{V_2}{2} \end{aligned} $$

Put the value of $V_1$ in equation (ii),

$$ h_{22}=\frac{I_2}{V_2}=\frac{15}{12}=1.25 \mathrm{~S} $$

Hence, the value of $h_{22}$ is $\mathbf{1 . 2 5 ~ S}$.

content:
The equivalent resistance in the infinite ladder network shown in the figure, is$${R_e}$$

The value of $${R_e}/R$$ is _______________
explanation:
Given circuit can be replaced by circuit shown below,



$${R_{eq}} = R + {{R \times {R_{eq}}} \over {R + {R_{eq}}}}$$

$${R_{eq}} = {{{R^2} + R{R_{eq}} + R{R_{eq}}} \over {(R + {R_{eq}})}}$$

$${R_{eq}}(R + {R_{eq}}) = {R^2} + R{R_{eq}} + R{R_{eq}}$$

$$R{R_{eq}} + R_{eq}^2 = {R^2} + 2R{R_{eq}}$$

$$R_{eq}^2 - R{R_{eq}} - {R^2} = 0$$

$${R_{eq}} = {{R \pm \sqrt {{R^2} + 4{R^2}} } \over 2} = {{R \pm \sqrt 5 R} \over 2}$$

Req cannot be negative. So,

$${R_{eq}} = \left[ {{{1 + \sqrt 5 } \over 2}} \right]R = 1.618R$$

Therefore, $${R_e} = R + {R_{eq}}$$

$${R_e} = R + 1.618R = 2.618R$$

$${{{R_e}} \over R} = 2.618 = 2.62$$

$$ \begin{aligned} Then\,\,& I_2=A_2=0 \mathrm{~A}, V_1=6 \mathrm{~V} \\ & I_1=A_1=4 \mathrm{~A}, V_2=6 \mathrm{~V} \end{aligned} $$

content:
Two two-port networks are connected in cascade. The combination is to be
represented as a single two-port network. The parameters of the network are
obtained by multiplying the individual
explanation:
When two two-port networks are connected in cascade, the overall (combined) two-port network parameters can be found as follows:

  • For cascade connection, the output port of the first network is connected to the input port of the second network.

  • In such a configuration, ABCD (Transmission) parameters are the most convenient, since for cascaded two-ports:

$ [T]_{overall} = [T]_1 \times [T]_2 $

Thus, the ABCD parameter matrices are multiplied to find the overall parameters of the cascaded network.

✅ Correct Option:

Option D — ABCD parameter matrix

Explanation:

  • z-parameters (impedance parameters): useful for series connections.

  • y-parameters (admittance parameters): useful for parallel connections.

  • h-parameters (hybrid parameters): useful when one port variable is a mix of voltage and current, e.g., transistor models.

  • ABCD-parameters: useful for cascade connections because they relate input and output variables linearly in a form that allows direct multiplication.

Final Answer: ✅ Option D — ABCD parameter matrix

Two two-port network A and B are connected in parallel. The combination is to be represented as a single two-port network. The parameters of this network are obtained by addition of the individual
explanation:
If two 2-port networks $A$ and $B$ are connected in parallel then equivalent $y$-parameter is the sum of individual $y$-parameters.

$$ [Y]=[Y]_A+[Y]_B $$

Hence, the correct option is (C).
